category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Ordinary Differential Equations/Systems of ODEs

info.text: CSIR NET Mathematics — ODEs: Systems of ODEs. MCQ, 20 questions covering linear systems, matrix exponential, phase plane, equilibria and stability, linearization, Lyapunov functions, and qualitative theory.

name.text: ODE39-Q01 Solution of linear system via eigenvalues

questiontext.text: Find the general solution of the system \(\mathbf{x}' = A\mathbf{x}\) where \(A = \begin{pmatrix}1&2\\2&1\end{pmatrix}\).

feedback.text: Eigenvalues: \(\det(A-\lambda I)=(1-\lambda)^2-4=0\), so \((1-\lambda)^2=4\), \(\lambda=3\) or \(\lambda=-1\). For \(\lambda_1=3\): \((A-3I)v=0\): \((-2,2;2,-2)v=0\), eigenvector \(v_1=(1,1)^T\). For \(\lambda_2=-1\): \((A+I)v=0\): \((2,2;2,2)v=0\), eigenvector \(v_2=(1,-1)^T\). General solution: \(\mathbf{x}=C_1e^{3t}(1,1)^T+C_2e^{-t}(1,-1)^T\).
Incorrect. The eigenvalues are \(\lambda=3\) and \(\lambda=-1\), not 1 and 2.
Incorrect. The eigenvectors are \((1,1)^T\) and \((1,-1)^T\), not \((2,1)^T\) and \((1,2)^T\).
Incorrect. This form is for repeated eigenvalues; here the eigenvalues 3 and \(-1\) are distinct.

questiontext.text: For the linear system \(\mathbf{x}' = A\mathbf{x}\) with eigenvalues \(\lambda_1 = 2\) and \(\lambda_2 = -3\), the equilibrium at the origin is a:

feedback.text: When eigenvalues have opposite signs (one positive, one negative), the equilibrium is a saddle point — unstable. Solutions along the eigenvector of the negative eigenvalue approach the origin, while solutions along the eigenvector of the positive eigenvalue flee. The stable manifold (along \(\lambda_2=-3\)) and unstable manifold (along \(\lambda_1=2\)) cross at the origin. Saddle points are always unstable.
Incorrect. A stable node requires both eigenvalues to have negative real parts.
Incorrect. Spirals arise from complex eigenvalues; these eigenvalues are real.
Incorrect. A center requires purely imaginary eigenvalues. Real eigenvalues of opposite signs give a saddle.

questiontext.text: Compute \(e^{At}\) for \(A = \begin{pmatrix}0&1\\-1&0\end{pmatrix}\).

feedback.text: The matrix \(A\) represents rotation. Eigenvalues: \(\lambda=\pm i\). Using \(A^2=-I\), \(A^3=-A\), \(A^4=I\): \(e^{At}=I+At+\frac{(At)^2}{2!}+\cdots=(I+\frac{-t^2}{2!}+\cdots)I+(t-\frac{t^3}{3!}+\cdots)A=\cos(t)I+\sin(t)A\). Thus \(e^{At}=\begin{pmatrix}\cos t&\sin t\\-\sin t&\cos t\end{pmatrix}\). This is a rotation matrix — the system \(\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}\) rotates the phase plane.
Incorrect. Diagonal matrix exponentials only work for diagonal matrices; \(A\) has imaginary eigenvalues, not real ones.
Incorrect. Hyperbolic functions arise for real eigenvalues; purely imaginary eigenvalues give trigonometric functions.
Incorrect. This form would arise for a nilpotent perturbation of a scalar matrix; here the eigenvalues are \(\pm i\).

questiontext.text: Consider the nonlinear system \(x' = -x + y^2\), \(y' = -y\). The origin is:

feedback.text: Linearization at origin: \(J=\begin{pmatrix}-1&0\\0&-1\end{pmatrix}\) (eigenvalues \(-1,-1\)), both negative — origin is stable for linearization. For the nonlinear system, try Lyapunov \(V=x^2+y^2\): \(\dot V=2x(-x+y^2)+2y(-y)=-2x^2+2xy^2-2y^2=-2(x^2+y^2)+2xy^2\). Near origin: \(|xy^2|\leq\frac{1}{2}(x^2+y^4)\leq\frac{1}{2}(x^2+y^2)^2\). So \(\dot V\leq-2(x^2+y^2)+C(x^2+y^2)^2<0\) for small \(r\). Origin is asymptotically stable.
Incorrect. Eigenvalues of the linearization are both \(-1\), indicating stability.

answer.text: Stable but not asymptotically stable (Lyapunov stable only)

feedback.text: Incorrect. With both linearization eigenvalues having negative real parts, the origin is asymptotically stable.
Incorrect. Linearization is inconclusive only when the real parts are zero; here both are \(-1\neq 0\).

questiontext.text: Solve the system \(\mathbf{x}' = \begin{pmatrix}2&1\\0&2\end{pmatrix}\mathbf{x}\).

feedback.text: Repeated eigenvalue \(\lambda=2\). Only one eigenvector: \(v_1=(1,0)^T\). Find generalized eigenvector \(v_2\): \((A-2I)v_2=v_1\), i.e., \(\begin{pmatrix}0&1\\0&0\end{pmatrix}v_2=(1,0)^T\): gives \(v_2=(0,1)^T\). General solution: \(\mathbf{x}=C_1e^{2t}v_1+C_2e^{2t}(tv_1+v_2)=C_1e^{2t}(1,0)^T+C_2e^{2t}[t(1,0)^T+(0,1)^T]\).
Incorrect. This only gives a 1D family; the full solution requires two independent solutions.
Incorrect. \((0,1)^T\) is not an eigenvector (\((A-2I)(0,1)^T=(1,0)^T\neq 0\)); it's a generalized eigenvector.
Incorrect. This would work only if both components evolved independently as \(e^{2t}\), ignoring the coupling term.

questiontext.text: The Lotka-Volterra system \(x' = ax - bxy\), \(y' = -cy + dxy\) (\(a,b,c,d>0\)) has a non-trivial equilibrium at:

feedback.text: Setting \(x'=y'=0\): \(ax-bxy=x(a-by)=0\) and \(-cy+dxy=y(-c+dx)=0\). Non-trivial: \(y=a/b\) and \(x=c/d\). The Jacobian at this point has purely imaginary eigenvalues (for the linearized system), suggesting a center — but the full nonlinear system has closed orbits (periodic solutions) by a conserved quantity \(H=dx-c\ln x+by-a\ln y=\text{const}\).
Incorrect. From \(a-by^*=0\): \(y^*=a/b\); from \(-c+dx^*=0\): \(x^*=c/d\).
Incorrect. These are the values swapped: \(x^*=c/d\) and \(y^*=a/b\).
Incorrect. This is the trivial equilibrium; the non-trivial one is \((c/d, a/b)\).

name.text: ODE39-Q07 Center vs. spiral — pure imaginary eigenvalues

questiontext.text: For the linear system with eigenvalues \(\lambda = \pm 2i\), the origin is classified as:

answer.text: A center (stable but not asymptotically stable; all orbits are closed ellipses)

feedback.text: Purely imaginary eigenvalues \(\lambda=\pm\beta i\) (real part zero) give a center for linear systems: solutions are periodic with period \(2\pi/\beta\), tracing ellipses around the origin. The origin is Lyapunov stable (nearby orbits stay nearby) but NOT asymptotically stable (orbits don't approach the origin). Caution: for nonlinear systems, the center can become a spiral (stable or unstable) depending on higher-order terms.
Incorrect. Unstable spiral requires \(\text{Re}(\lambda)>0\); here \(\text{Re}(\pm 2i)=0\).
Incorrect. Stable spiral requires \(\text{Re}(\lambda)<0\); here the real part is zero.
Incorrect. Saddle requires real eigenvalues of opposite sign; here the eigenvalues are purely imaginary.

questiontext.text: A fundamental matrix \(\Phi(t)\) for the system \(\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}\) satisfies \(\Phi'=A\Phi\) and \(\det\Phi(t)\neq 0\). The solution with initial condition \(\mathbf{x}(0)=\mathbf{x}_0\) is:

feedback.text: The general solution of \(\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}\) is \(\mathbf{x}(t)=\Phi(t)\mathbf{c}\) for arbitrary constant vector \(\mathbf{c}\). Applying the initial condition: \(\mathbf{x}(0)=\Phi(0)\mathbf{c}=\mathbf{x}_0\), so \(\mathbf{c}=\Phi(0)^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0\). Thus \(\mathbf{x}(t)=\Phi(t)\Phi(0)^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0\). If we choose the principal fundamental matrix \(\Phi(0)=I\) (i.e., \(\Phi=e^{At}\)), then \(\mathbf{x}(t)=e^{At}\mathbf{x}_0\).
Incorrect. This would be correct only if \(\Phi(0)=I\) (the principal fundamental matrix); in general, we need \(\Phi(0)^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0\).
Incorrect. The solution is expressed via \(\Phi(t)\), not \(A\Phi(t)\).
Incorrect. The order of factors is reversed; the correct formula is \(\Phi(t)\Phi(0)^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0\).

name.text: ODE39-Q09 Phase portrait — stable vs unstable node

questiontext.text: For a 2D linear system with eigenvalues \(\lambda_1 = -1\) and \(\lambda_2 = -4\) (both negative, distinct), the equilibrium is a:

answer.text: Stable node (all trajectories approach the origin as \(t\to+\infty\))

feedback.text: Both eigenvalues are real and negative. All solutions \(\mathbf{x}=C_1e^{-t}\mathbf{v}_1+C_2e^{-4t}\mathbf{v}_2\to\mathbf{0}\) as \(t\to\infty\). This is a stable node (also called an attracting node). For large \(t\), the slower-decaying mode \(e^{-t}\) dominates, so most trajectories approach the origin tangentially to the eigenvector \(\mathbf{v}_1\) of the eigenvalue with smaller magnitude.
Incorrect. Unstable node requires both eigenvalues to have positive real parts.
Incorrect. Saddle requires eigenvalues of opposite signs; here both are negative.
Incorrect. Centers arise from purely imaginary eigenvalues; here the eigenvalues are real negative.

questiontext.text: The Poincaré-Bendixson theorem (for 2D systems) states that a bounded orbit of a smooth autonomous system in \(\mathbb{R}^2\) that doesn't approach an equilibrium must:

answer.text: Approach a limit cycle (a closed periodic orbit)

feedback.text: Poincaré-Bendixson theorem: if a trajectory of a smooth planar autonomous system is bounded and its omega-limit set contains no equilibrium, then the omega-limit set is a periodic orbit (limit cycle). This is a powerful dichotomy for 2D systems: bounded trajectories either approach equilibria or limit cycles. It fails in 3D (where chaotic attractors can exist, e.g., Lorenz system). Applications: proving existence of periodic orbits by constructing a trapping region.
Incorrect. The hypothesis is that the orbit is bounded; the conclusion concerns its limiting behavior.
Incorrect. Chaos cannot occur in 2D autonomous systems (Poincaré-Bendixson rules it out); chaos requires at least 3D.
Incorrect. The hypothesis excludes convergence to equilibria; the conclusion is a periodic orbit, not necessarily the origin.

questiontext.text: Lyapunov's theorem for asymptotic stability: if there exists a function \(V(\mathbf{x})\) (Lyapunov function) near an equilibrium \(\mathbf{0}\) with \(V(\mathbf{0})=0\), \(V(\mathbf{x})>0\) for \(\mathbf{x} eq\mathbf{0}\), and \(\dot V = abla V\cdot f < 0\) for \(\mathbf{x} eq\mathbf{0}\), then:

feedback.text: Lyapunov's second method: if a positive definite \(V\) exists with \(\dot V<0\) (negative definite), then the equilibrium is asymptotically stable. If \(\dot V\leq 0\) (negative semi-definite), Lyapunov stability (but not necessarily asymptotic). Lyapunov's instability theorem: if \(\dot V>0\) for some \(V\) with \(V(\mathbf{0})=0\), then instability. The method is global when applied on all of \(\mathbb{R}^n\) (Barbalat's lemma extends to non-autonomous systems).

answer.text: The equilibrium is only Lyapunov stable (not asymptotically)

feedback.text: Incorrect. With \(\dot V<0\) (strict), the conclusion is asymptotic stability, not just Lyapunov stability.
Incorrect. Instability requires \(\dot V>0\) for some \(V\); here \(\dot V<0\) implies stability.

answer.text: The conclusion is inconclusive without knowing \(V\) explicitly

feedback.text: Incorrect. Lyapunov's theorem gives a definitive conclusion from the existence of such a function \(V\).

name.text: ODE39-Q12 Variation of parameters for systems

questiontext.text: The solution of \(\mathbf{x}' = A\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{g}(t)\) with fundamental matrix \(\Phi(t)\) is given by the variation of constants formula:

feedback.text: Variation of constants (Duhamel's principle): look for \(\mathbf{x}=\Phi\mathbf{u}(t)\). Then \(\Phi\mathbf{u}'+\Phi'\mathbf{u}=A\Phi\mathbf{u}+\mathbf{g}\). Since \(\Phi'=A\Phi\): \(\Phi\mathbf{u}'=\mathbf{g}\), so \(\mathbf{u}'=\Phi^{-1}\mathbf{g}\) and \(\mathbf{u}=\mathbf{c}+\int_0^t\Phi^{-1}(s)\mathbf{g}(s)ds\). With \(\Phi=e^{At}\): \(\mathbf{x}(t)=e^{At}\mathbf{x}_0+\int_0^te^{A(t-s)}\mathbf{g}(s)ds\) (convolution with matrix exponential).
Incorrect. The variation of constants formula involves the product \(\Phi(t)\Phi^{-1}(s)\mathbf{g}(s)\) inside the integral.
Incorrect. The inverse fundamental matrix \(\Phi^{-1}(s)\) must appear inside the integral.
Incorrect. The homogeneous part is \(\Phi(t)\mathbf{c}\) (not \(\Phi^{-1}\mathbf{c}\)).

questiontext.text: The Van der Pol oscillator \(x'' - \mu(1-x^2)x' + x = 0\) (\(\mu>0\)) exhibits:

answer.text: A unique stable limit cycle surrounding the unstable equilibrium at the origin

feedback.text: The Van der Pol oscillator: for \(|x|<1\) the damping \(-\mu(1-x^2)<0\) (negative damping, energy injection), and for \(|x|>1\) the damping \(-\mu(1-x^2)>0\) (positive damping, energy dissipation). This leads to a unique stable limit cycle (by Liénard's theorem). The origin is unstable (linearization eigenvalues have positive real part \(\mu/2\)). All trajectories (except the origin) spiral toward the limit cycle. For small \(\mu\): nearly sinusoidal oscillation. For large \(\mu\): relaxation oscillations.

answer.text: A stable equilibrium at the origin

feedback.text: Incorrect. The origin is unstable for \(\mu>0\): linearization gives eigenvalues with positive real part \(\mu/2\).
Incorrect. The Van der Pol oscillator is a classic example of a system with a limit cycle.
Incorrect. As a 2D system, Van der Pol cannot be chaotic (Poincaré-Bendixson); it has a limit cycle even for large \(\mu\).

name.text: ODE39-Q14 Conversion to system of first order

questiontext.text: The ODE \(y''' + 2y'' - y' + 3y = f(x)\) can be written as a first-order system \(\mathbf{u}' = A\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{b}\) with state vector \(\mathbf{u}=(y, y', y'')^T\). The matrix \(A\) is:

feedback.text: With \(u_1=y\), \(u_2=y'\), \(u_3=y''\): \(u_1'=u_2\), \(u_2'=u_3\), \(u_3'=y'''=-2y''+y'-3y=-2u_3+u_2-3u_1\). This gives the companion matrix (Frobenius form): \(A=\begin{pmatrix}0&1&0\\0&0&1\\-3&1&-2\end{pmatrix}\). The last row contains the negatives of the ODE coefficients: for \(y'''+2y''-y'+3y=0\), the row is \((-3,1,-2)\). The characteristic polynomial of \(A\) equals the characteristic polynomial of the ODE: \(\lambda^3+2\lambda^2-\lambda+3\).
Incorrect. The sign: \(u_3'=-2u_3+u_2-3u_1\), so the last row is \((-3,1,-2)\).
Incorrect. The companion matrix form has 0s and 1s in the upper rows and ODE coefficients in the last row.
Incorrect. The companion matrix for an \(n\)th-order ODE has the identity in the first \(n-1\) superdiagonal and ODE coefficients in the last row.

questiontext.text: Bendixson's criterion: for the planar system \(x'=f(x,y)\), \(y'=g(x,y)\), if \(\text{div}(f,g)=\partial f/\partial x + \partial g/\partial y\) is of one sign (and nonzero) in a simply connected region \(D\), then:

answer.text: There are no closed orbits (limit cycles) in \(D\)

feedback.text: Bendixson's criterion: if \(\text{div}(f,g)=f_x+g_y\) doesn't change sign and is not identically zero on a simply connected region \(D\), then there are no closed orbits in \(D\). Proof: by Green's theorem, if a closed orbit \(\Gamma\) exists in \(D\), then \(\oint_\Gamma(f\,dy-g\,dx)=\iint_\text{interior}(f_x+g_y)\,dA\). But \(\oint(f\,dy-g\,dx)=0\) (since \((f,g)\) is tangent to \(\Gamma\)). Contradiction if \(f_x+g_y\) has one sign. Extension: Dulac's criterion uses a multiplier function.
Incorrect. Bendixson's criterion only says no closed orbits exist; it doesn't specify where orbits go.

answer.text: The system has a unique equilibrium in \(D\)

feedback.text: Incorrect. Bendixson's criterion concerns closed orbits, not the number of equilibria.
Incorrect. Chaos is impossible in 2D autonomous systems regardless; Bendixson rules out limit cycles.

name.text: ODE39-Q16 Trace and determinant for 2x2 system stability

questiontext.text: For a 2D linear system \(\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}\), with \(\text{tr}(A)=\tau\) and \(\det(A)=\delta\), the equilibrium is asymptotically stable iff:

feedback.text: Eigenvalues satisfy \(\lambda^2-\tau\lambda+\delta=0\): sum \(\lambda_1+\lambda_2=\tau\), product \(\lambda_1\lambda_2=\delta\). For asymptotic stability, both eigenvalues must have negative real parts: \(\text{Re}(\lambda_i)<0\). For real eigenvalues: both negative iff \(\tau<0\) (sum negative) and \(\delta>0\) (product positive). For complex eigenvalues: \(\text{Re}(\lambda)=\tau/2<0\) iff \(\tau<0\), and complex roots occur when \(\delta>0\) anyway. Summary: AS iff \(\tau<0\) AND \(\delta>0\) (Routh-Hurwitz for 2×2).
Incorrect. \(\delta<0\) means eigenvalues have opposite signs (one positive, one negative), giving a saddle — unstable.
Incorrect. \(\tau>0\) means at least one eigenvalue has positive real part — unstable.
Incorrect. \(\delta>0\) (eigenvalues same sign or complex) is necessary but not sufficient; we also need \(\tau<0\) (sum of eigenvalues negative).

questiontext.text: A Hamiltonian system in 2D is \(x' = \partial H/\partial y\), \(y' = -\partial H/\partial x\). The divergence of the vector field \((f,g)=(\partial H/\partial y, -\partial H/\partial x)\) equals:

feedback.text: \(\text{div}(f,g)=\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial y}\right)+\frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(-\frac{\partial H}{\partial x}\right)=\frac{\partial^2 H}{\partial x\partial y}-\frac{\partial^2 H}{\partial y\partial x}=0\) (by equality of mixed partials). This means Hamiltonian systems are volume-preserving (Liouville's theorem) and by Bendixson's criterion cannot have limit cycles — only centers. Energy \(H(x,y)=\text{const}\) along trajectories: \(\frac{dH}{dt}=H_x x'+H_y y'=H_x H_y-H_y H_x=0\).
Incorrect. The divergence of the Hamiltonian vector field is exactly zero, as shown by equality of mixed partials.
Incorrect. The divergence of the Hamiltonian field is zero; \(|\nabla H|^2\) is the squared gradient magnitude.
Incorrect. The divergence of any Hamiltonian vector field is identically zero.

questiontext.text: The Hartman-Grobman theorem states that near a hyperbolic equilibrium \(\mathbf{x}_0\) of \(\mathbf{x}'=f(\mathbf{x})\):

answer.text: The nonlinear flow is topologically conjugate to the flow of the linearization \(\mathbf{x}'=Df(\mathbf{x}_0)\mathbf{x}\)

feedback.text: Hartman-Grobman: if all eigenvalues of \(Df(\mathbf{x}_0)\) have nonzero real parts (hyperbolic equilibrium), then locally the nonlinear flow is topologically conjugate (homeomorphically equivalent, preserving orbits and time direction) to the linear flow. This justifies linearization for stability classification: stable/unstable/saddle character is preserved. The theorem fails for non-hyperbolic equilibria (e.g., centers can become spirals due to nonlinear terms). For smooth conjugacy (not just topological), one needs non-resonance conditions.

answer.text: The nonlinear and linear flows are identical (equal, not just topologically equivalent)

feedback.text: Incorrect. The theorem gives topological equivalence (homeomorphism conjugacy), not equality; nonlinear terms change the quantitative behavior.

answer.text: The equilibrium is always a stable node near a hyperbolic point

feedback.text: Incorrect. The theorem applies to any hyperbolic equilibrium (stable/unstable nodes, saddles, spirals); it doesn't assert stability.

answer.text: The theorem applies only when the Jacobian is symmetric

feedback.text: Incorrect. The theorem requires hyperbolicity (all eigenvalues have nonzero real parts), not symmetry of the Jacobian.

questiontext.text: For a hyperbolic equilibrium of \(\mathbf{x}'=f(\mathbf{x})\) with \(k\) eigenvalues with negative real part and \(n-k\) with positive real part, the stable manifold \(W^s\) has dimension:

answer.text: \(k\) (equal to the number of stable eigenvalues)

feedback.text: The Stable Manifold Theorem: the stable manifold \(W^s=\{\mathbf{x}: \phi_t(\mathbf{x})\to\mathbf{0}\text{ as }t\to+\infty\}\) is a smooth \(k\)-dimensional invariant manifold tangent at the equilibrium to the stable eigenspace \(E^s\) of \(Df(\mathbf{0})\). Similarly, the unstable manifold \(W^u\) has dimension \(n-k\). For a saddle in 2D (\(k=1, n-k=1\)): stable and unstable manifolds are 1D curves. The center manifold theorem handles the remaining \(n_c\) eigenvalues with zero real part.

answer.text: \(n-k\) (equal to the number of unstable eigenvalues)

feedback.text: Incorrect. The STABLE manifold has dimension \(k\) (number of stable eigenvalues); the unstable manifold has dimension \(n-k\).
Incorrect. The stable manifold is full-dimensional only if all eigenvalues are stable (\(k=n\)); in that case it's a neighborhood of the equilibrium.

answer.text: 0 (the stable manifold is just the equilibrium point)

feedback.text: Incorrect. The stable manifold includes all trajectories converging to the equilibrium; it has dimension equal to the number of stable eigenvalues.

name.text: ODE39-Q20 Invariant manifolds — center manifold reduction

questiontext.text: The center manifold theorem is used when the linearization of \(\mathbf{x}'=f(\mathbf{x})\) at an equilibrium has eigenvalues with zero real part. The theorem guarantees:

answer.text: An invariant center manifold \(W^c\) tangent to the center eigenspace, on which the stability behavior of the full system is determined

feedback.text: Center Manifold Theorem: if the linearization has \(n_s\) stable, \(n_c\) center, and \(n_u\) unstable eigenvalues, there exists a local invariant center manifold \(W^c\) of dimension \(n_c\), tangent to the center eigenspace \(E^c\). The stability of the equilibrium is determined by the reduced dynamics on \(W^c\). If \(n_u=0\), the equilibrium is stable iff the origin of the center manifold system is stable. This reduces the stability question to a lower-dimensional problem (from \(n\) to \(n_c\) dimensions).

answer.text: The equilibrium is always unstable when center eigenvalues exist

feedback.text: Incorrect. Center eigenvalues are inconclusive for stability; it depends on the reduced dynamics on \(W^c\).

answer.text: The full system is equivalent to a purely conservative (Hamiltonian) system

feedback.text: Incorrect. The center manifold theorem doesn't imply Hamiltonian structure; it's a local dimensionality reduction tool.

answer.text: The center manifold is unique and globally defined

feedback.text: Incorrect. Center manifolds are generally local (not global) and need not be unique; they may not even be smooth beyond a certain order.
